sentences.text: Fritz Gluckstein.
F-R-I-T-Z G-L-U-C-K-S-T-E-I-N.
My-- at birth was Fritz Gluckstein, an umlaut over the U.
Yes.
My full name is Fritz Paul Julius Herman.
And at one time, it was Israel, too.
No, they were not used.
Only the name Israel had to be used later.
January 24, 1927.
70 years old.
In Berlin.
I grew up in Berlin, yes.
My father, my mother, and we had a maid.
Frieda.
No, she came when I was-- I still remember her coming.
I must have been three years old.
I remember just, even remember her dress.
But about three years old, I would imagine.
She remained, I would say, til 1933, when Hitler came to power.
Yes.
Yes, at that time-- number one, my father lost his job, and we couldn't afford a maid.
And number two, the Nuremberg Laws prohibited the employment of any help under the age of 45.
My father's name was Georg.
George without an E.
The same, right.
My father was a judge.
Actually, very-- he'd give a somewhat stern appearance, particularly whenever you wanted to take a picture of him.
He immediately takes a position.
And of course, I have that from him.
But he was a judge, but he wasn't judgmental.
Had a great sense of humor.
And actually, I enjoyed being with him.
I enjoyed meeting his colleagues.
I grew among lawyers, and I still remember-- our birthdays are very close together.
He was born on the 27th of January.
And I remember, I had my birthday, and then, a few days later, his birthday.
And I never could figure out, grown men, they sit in a big room, smoke cigars, drink something.
There are no games.
I could come in and say good night, and hello, but actually, it was very nice.
And I still remember one of my father's colleague, who didn't-- when you met him, he didn't say hello, how are you.
He said, what do you have to say in your defense?
I still remember it.
And Jewish lawyers, non-Jewish lawyers, a few engineers, too, but primarily lawyers.
And what sort of things did you like to do with your father?
Oh, we used to go on hikes.
And my father had a map, and he showed me exactly, here we are on the map.
And I still remember, my father was actually very-- he never would be seen without a coat.
But on rare occasions, like [INAUDIBLE] did our hikes, he really didn't have a coat.
He even-- I remember my father, the judge-- well, we couldn't-- there was a fence, and a hole in the fence, and he actually slipped through the fence.
It impressed me.
So he had the sense of humor, and the when necessary, he knew how to unbend.
Yes, from the beginning, and later on in school.
Every time I came home, every night, now, what was going on.
What was covered.
Were you asked, did you ever recite, what did you answer.
Any tests, or so on.
And I remember later on, I might go ahead, it was-- I didn't do a good job in mathematics, it was a bad test.
Lo and behold, we went to the synagogue that night, and who was sitting in front of us?
My math teacher.
It occurred to me now, I told my father there was no test, so that he didn't know about it.
Now, what would happen if he were to ask the mathematics teacher, well, how did he do?
I've got to say, of course, that it was Shabbat, amd it wasn't discussed, and of course nothing happened.
No, it was very important.
He always kept that.
Did you eat meals together as a family?
Dinner?
Yes, but if I recall, regularly my father was at several courts at the same time.
The [? liebercourt ?].
And very often he came home late.
But I remember some meals, but not regularly.
Not very.
But he was a religious man, absolutely.
I was raised Jewish from the first day on, and we went to synagogue regularly.
We made the kiddush.
Usually a candle were lit, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the blessings.
In fact, whenever I hear the blessings now, I still hear them in German like my father did it.
But ?] frequently in synagogues, you see in Berlin some different setup.
There's one-- there was one overall congregation, as such.
And within the congregation we had the conservative, the liberal, and the reform.
Conservatives would see what we have, perhaps a little bit more orthodox than the conservative here.
The liberal was in between the reform and conservative.
There you had an organ, but women and men are separated, except one synagogue where they are together.
And then you had the reform.
No head covering, and services was primarily in German.
But he actually didn't belong to synagogue.
Usually you went to the synagogue closest to you, but of course, the rabbis, each synagogue had a rabbi.
But the rabbis took turns.
Once a month, they went elsewhere.
The same with the cantors.
Each synagogue had actually two cantors, and they took turns, too.
I remember my father selecting the rabbis that he liked to hear.
And I remember some sermons I enjoyed.
Some I didn't understand.
And some I was bored to tears.
I remember one rabbi, it was Dr.
Weiner. ?] He was a field rabbi during the war.
My father knew him from the war.
And he was pretty good, but he used the word noteworthy, or the equivalent in German, and I was sitting there and counting, and I told him today you used it 6 times, or 7 times.
But we enjoyed going to the synagogue.
I enjoyed the singing.
Usually he went to liberal, but occasionally my father went to orthodox.
I would say conservative.
The orthodox kept themself apart.
They actually belong to the congregation, but they kept themself apart.
They had their synagogue in a certain part of town, and the Orthodox Jews lived close by.
They called them orthodox, we considered them ultra orthodox.
What conservative, we considered orthodox, but this are semantics.
Yes, but of course over the years-- when-- I was young, but.
Yes, I remember the famous Rabbi Nussbaum.
He was-- he had certain mannerisms, but he was very dramatic.
He came to the United States.
Then there was the Rabbi Prince.
He had one-- in his synagogue, men and women sat together.
And there was one rabbi who-- well, my father didn't like him because the sermons were impossible.
However, he was an excellent administrator.
He administered the old people's home, and that was his forte.
Zalamunski. ?] And then of course, I remember the rabbi for my bar mitzvah.
Oberlander. ?]
Yes.
In fact, I remember one certain service and in fact I went-- at the wedding of the Rabbi [?
Oberlander, ?] it was performed by Baeck.
Baeck-- this I heard from my father-- was a very learned rabbi.
His sermons were quite-- many people didn't understand what he was.
I remember, I didn't quite follow him, either.
But he was highly respected.
There was no chief rabbi in Berlin.
It didn't exist.
But he was the chief rabbi, actually.
He was quite-- his personality.
In fact, you could feel when you saw him, he was quite-- you could see that he was very distinct.
Even I could, as a little boy, could see.
Probably feel it, how he was treated.
With reverence.
Yes.
Yes.
Absolutely.
And I learned quite the liturgy.
Oh yes.
Yes.
You see the [INAUDIBLE] prayer book, Hebrew one side, German on the other, and yes, he pointed out.
Yes, I went-- while I was in grade school, even after the takeover by the Nazis.
We had religious instruction, and then later on, I went regularly-- I don't recall, once or twice a week to [INAUDIBLE] school during the day.
The afternoons.
History.
At one time, I knew all the kings of Judah and Israel.
I knew all by heart.
No longer.
And Hebrew, too.
Unfortunately, probably have forgotten more Hebrew than I ever knew.
But an effort was made to teach us Hebrew at the beginning.
I remember the rabbi who taught us.
And then once he said, who can tell me how do you pronounce [HEBREW], not [HEBREW].
And I knew that, and he gave me a dime for this, I remember it.
It was quite something.
Mm.
Probably not very good, but not bad, either.
But to be perfectly honest, we didn't like to go particularly to afternoons.
Go to religious school, not be [INAUDIBLE].
What happened in the public school.
At the beginning they came to our school.
Then we had to go to another public school close by, they combined.
Let's say there were three in the area-- precinct with three public schools, and all the Jewish student met at a particular school for the religious class.
That was-- [INAUDIBLE] time was taken-- we were given time.
Even at the beginning, we actually took part at the Christian instruction.
I don't know how that happened, but I remember he was very interesting.
But beyond that, that was it.
Yes.
It just happened.
I believe at that reason, either the teacher didn't come or it wasn't set up yet.
It happened to be they had religious instruction, [INAUDIBLE] good.
You didn't wander around in German school out in the hall.
Unheard of.
Therefore you'd just [? chatter. ?] And I found it quite interesting, what he had to say.
But beyond that, it was mere curiosity.
No.
Actually, this was given.
That was later.
And I would say there were about four or five or even six in the class.
When I [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's, of course, after.
In fact, I went to school after the take overby the Nazis.
My mother's name was Hedwig.
H-E-D-W-I-G.
Heinrich.
H-E-I-N-R-I-C-H.
I think she was a very beautiful woman.
I remember she had very long hair.
Very long hair, and wore it in a bun.
And I've been told that she didn't like it at that time. it was old fashioned, you know.
But my father insisted.
However, she got an ally and a good friend.
He was a doctor.
His name was [?
Hillovkoski. ?] Later on he was the chief of gynecology at the Jewish hospital.
And he said, well, for health reasons, the hair is too heavy, OK, and therefore, they had to take it off.
Now remember, the day my mother came with short hair.
I liked it.
Well, he was outvoted.
I don't know exactly what happened with the [INAUDIBLE].
I was told afterwards that he was quite against it.
Well, my father one time went down one of the main thoroughfares in Berlin and passed a bank, with a young lady trying to get the marquee up and down, it was stuck.
He helped her, let me help you, and that's the way they met.
Excellent, that's correct.
No, they don't.
I know that, of course, some eyebrows were raised for the families, but only temporarily.
And I must--
Well, of course, usually marrying a Jewish person-- not to be ultra anti-Semitic, but even there, Protestant married Protestant, Catholic married Catholics, and Jews married Jews.
What's the order there?
My mother was Protestant.
Lutheran, what they call in Germany they have it either Catholic or Evangelical, Evangelisch, that's what they call it, but in effect it's Lutheran.
But of course I was told I guess my father's side, particularly Aunt Bertha who was my father's aunt, Was somewhat surprised too, but after she met my mother she was accepted.
It was actually yes, one of my father's cousin, not many relatives left, but she was, Aunt Bertha, yes.
Baumgard. ?]

In fact, I was being told when I was born one uncle had to go out, get a big cab, to she was very particular, didn't like to go by car, the biggest cab, and she rode down to the hospital to visit my mother.
On the other side, of course, even if eyebrows were raised, my father was a judge.
And that was quite-- at that time a judge was a very good position and therefore.
But the way I understand the eyebrow raising was only temporary, and everything worked out.
Quite well.
And once, wondered perhaps in the beginning.
But they were very nice, and in fact, I can't complain, I got Christmas presents.
We had actually a Christmas tree at Christmas time.
And of course I had friends and non-Jewish friends and relatives who went to Christmas parties.
It was very fine.
And we occasionally went to church.
I found it very interesting, you could watch the organist.
And while there was no cantor, there was singing.
And I actually enjoyed going.
Once in awhile.
Occasionally at Christmas, very nice, even now I like to go to Christmas service.
And the sermon I don't recall too much, but I enjoy a sermon.
A good sermon any denomination, even now I enjoy it.
I remember the big church was quite-- people singing-- something different, but of course the organist really fascinated me.
They have an organ in the liberal Synagogue too but you couldn't see it, but I could stand right next to organist and watch him.
And I was seeing the number of songs that posted, you see, and everybody knew.
It was quite interesting.
No, she wasn't religious.
But she went to church occasionally, but [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she probably would have converted, but my father said he didn't want it.
You were raised, born Lutheran.
I don't want you to change.
Yes.
Actually in Germany, you have to have a civil ceremony.
That is what counts.
Legal is civil.
But afterwords you can go to religious ceremony, but you have to have a civil ceremony.
Yes, but at that time my father was the head of the family, he presented the family, therefore the children were to be raised Jewish.
That's how it was done.
Yes.
And I remember what happened, were several physicians present.
And I was told one of the physicians went home, and his wife thought he was elsewhere and shot him.
Mistaken.
Yes certainly, you could tell he was out of breath.
The woman was disturbed, actually.
Right, when I was six years old, 1933, I entered the neighborhood school.
You more or less had to go there.
If you wanted to go elsewhere, you had special permission.
1933, [INAUDIBLE] my father, I remember when I went to school, my father still had the nice apartment.
I got my first report card still in the nice apartment, then we had to move.
First we lived [COUGH] in a very nice apartment.
I remember, I was six years old.
But it didn't have hot water, and it didn't have central heating.
Of course, that wouldn't do, and we moved to a very nice apartment in a different part of town which had all those conveniences.
I remember of course, even had a maid there.
But it was very close.
I remember actually we were out, on a-- we went out summer, took an apartment in the suburbs away at the lakes.
And I remember vaguely where father got the notice that he was dismissed.
I remember that vaguely.
But--
Yes.
Not right when he came to power.
It was January, but quite early.
When I went--
No.
Not at all, no.
Actually there were very few, I would see the Western part-- let's put it this way.
The Western part of Berlin had the upper and middle class Jews, the central eastern part had the blue collar worker and the people that had immigrated from the east.
But there was no ghetto as such.
I would say yes.
Not the really, there was some actually where they had houses called villa, as we have here, but still apartment.
In order to live in a house, you were considered quite affluent.
But we had a very nice apartment.
Yes, absolutely.
No, unfortunately not.
Well, very simple.
A study or gentleman's room, living room, more or less [INAUDIBLE] living room or eating room, then bedroom, room for the children, and a room for the maid.
That was usually-- some had more.
Yes of course.
Yes.
As I recall, very nice.
Yes, very nice.

Of course, we lived on the top floor and we made a lot of noise, and sometimes they complained, but they were very nice, and they made it very clear when we left they hated us, hated our leaving it, I remember.
Even gave me candy, and it was very nice.
Right around the corner, [INAUDIBLE] a smaller apartment in the courtyard.
You had to go through the front building into the courtyard.
And fewer rooms but still very nice.
And so most of my time, six years till we had to move out there I spent there.
And same school, it was nice too, I could go to the same school.
That was later on we had to leave it.
That was quite a bit-- that was actually after the war started.
We were compelled to leave it.
Or found it wise to do so, I'm quite sure.
No, we couldn't take them at all.
Well actually, grade school I didn't have any favorite subjects.
But later on in high school yes, but actually in grade school, and actually was extremely fortunate with that grade school or public school.
I only can report what happened to me, but the teacher, the homeroom teacher, still his name was [?
Schulze ?] a party member, the emblem right here, not once did he make an anti-Semitic remark.
He didn't treat us six or seven Jewish students any different.
Absolutely.
I must say the man was, his behavior was impeccable.
Of course the school was such, it was fortunately the principal-- his name [? was Hinzer ?]-- kept things really in order.
Quite sure, of course, I can only guess, that he tried his best to keep the Nazis out.
But I must say, I have only pleasant memories of school.
Not once did I hear an anti-Semitic remark.
Later on, however, certain students had to be admitted, and they started really quite anti-Semitic, calling names and harassment.
And actually my parents went to see the principal, and he made it clear that he simply cannot dismiss, he can't expel him.
At that time, I left the public school.
Officially I wasn't required to do so, but the principal said I can't, I did what I-- it would be better if I would leave.
He did not-- there was no Nazi teachers because the principal more or less could determine what teacher he wanted, but the students he could not do.
